segments.title: Sectionalism
segments.subtitle: How slavery split a country in two

sections.heading: Two Different Economies

content.simplified:
In the early 1800s, the United States was one country. But the North and the South lived very different lives. People started to care more about their region than the whole country. This idea is called sectionalism.

The biggest difference was how people made money. The North had cold winters and rocky soil. Northerners built factories. They ran small farms. They traded in big cities. Most workers got paid for their jobs.

The South had warm weather and rich soil. Farmers could grow cotton, tobacco, and sugar. These crops sold all over the world. But growing them needed a lot of workers. Southern farms, called plantations, used enslaved Black people for the work. This system is called the plantation economy. It was built on slavery.

Slavery was cruel. Enslaved people were treated like property. They could be bought, sold, or beaten. They worked all day for no pay. By 1860, about four million enslaved people lived in the South.

Some people thought slavery would end on its own. But in 1793, a man named Eli Whitney invented the cotton gin. This machine cleaned cotton fifty times faster than a person could. Cotton became very profitable. Plantation owners wanted more enslaved workers, not fewer.

By the 1820s, states north of the Mason Dixon Line were free states. Slavery was illegal there. States south of the line were slave states. People used the line to talk about the split between two ways of life.
content.standard:
By the early 1800s, the United States looked like one country on a map but felt like two different ones in real life. The North and the South had grown apart so much that people began to think of them as separate places with separate ways of life. This growing loyalty to a region instead of to the whole country had a name: sectionalism.

Most of the difference came down to how each region made its money. The North had cold winters, rocky soil, and lots of fast moving rivers that could power machines. So Northerners built factories, started small farms, and traded goods in busy port cities like New York and Boston. Their economy was built on free workers who got paid wages and could quit if they wanted to.

The South had warm weather, rich soil, and long growing seasons. That meant it could grow crops like cotton, tobacco, sugar, and rice that the rest of the world wanted to buy. But these crops needed huge amounts of labor. Southern landowners built large farms called plantations and forced enslaved Black people to do the work. This way of running the economy is called the plantation economy, and it was built on slavery.

Slavery was the cruelest part of American life. Enslaved people were treated as property. They could be bought, sold, beaten, or separated from their families. They worked from sunrise to sunset and were never paid. By 1860, about four million enslaved people lived in the United States, almost all of them in the South.

For a while, some Americans believed slavery would slowly fade away on its own. Then in 1793, a Northerner named Eli Whitney invented the cotton gin, a machine that cleaned cotton seeds about fifty times faster than a person could by hand. Cotton suddenly became one of the most profitable crops in the world. Plantation owners planted more of it, which meant they wanted more enslaved workers, not fewer. A machine that was supposed to save labor ended up trapping more people in slavery.

By the 1820s, states north of the Mason Dixon Line had mostly become free states, where slavery was illegal. States south of it became slave states. The line itself had been drawn back in colonial times to settle a land argument, but Americans started using it as shorthand for the divide between two ways of life.
content.advanced:
By the early 1800s, the United States was outwardly a single nation but functionally two distinct societies, each with its own labor system, economy, and self conception. Historians call this divergence sectionalism: the gradual replacement of national identity with regional identity, the slow but steady belief that being a Northerner or a Southerner mattered more than being an American.

The economic foundation of the divide was geography. The Northeast had been blessed with poor farmland, abundant waterpower, and a temperate climate suited to small scale agriculture. By the 1820s, Northerners were channeling these conditions into a new kind of economy: textile mills along New England rivers, ironworks in Pennsylvania, shipping merchants in Boston and New York, family farms producing for urban markets. Crucially, this economy ran on free wage labor. Workers could quit, move, organize, or save toward owning their own farms or shops.

The South pursued a fundamentally different economic strategy. With long growing seasons and rich soil, planters concentrated on cash crops for export: tobacco, rice, sugar, and above all cotton. These crops were extraordinarily labor intensive. Rather than relying on free wage labor, the South built its prosperity on the plantation economy, an agricultural system entirely dependent on the unpaid forced labor of enslaved Black people. By 1860, about four million people were enslaved in the United States, almost all in the South. Slavery was not a peripheral institution to the Southern economy. It was the system itself.

The condition of enslaved people was deliberate, total, and brutal. Enslaved Black Americans were classified as property under Southern law. They could be bought, sold, beaten, or separated from family without legal recourse. They worked from sunrise to sunset, and the children they bore inherited their condition. Resistance, when it occurred, was met with violence and tighter laws.

Many founding generation Americans had assumed slavery would gradually wither as it became economically irrational. The introduction of the cotton gin by Eli Whitney in 1793 destroyed that assumption. The gin separated cotton seeds from fiber roughly fifty times faster than hand labor, transforming cotton from a marginal crop into the most profitable commodity in the world. Demand for enslaved labor surged rather than declined. Between 1790 and 1860, the enslaved population grew from about 700,000 to nearly 4 million. A technological innovation that could have ended slavery instead expanded and entrenched it, a sobering example of how the moral consequences of technology depend on the systems that adopt it.

By the 1820s, the geographic boundary between freedom and slavery was firmly drawn. States north of the Mason Dixon Line (originally surveyed in the 1760s to settle a colonial property dispute) had abolished slavery and become free states. States to the south remained slave states. Americans began invoking the line not as a property boundary but as a shorthand for two incompatible systems and the moral question they posed: could a republic founded on the proposition that all men are created equal coexist indefinitely with a system that treated millions of human beings as property?

sections.keyIdea: The North built its economy on factories, free labor, and small farms, while the South built its economy on plantations worked by enslaved people. The cotton gin made cotton hugely profitable and locked slavery deeper into Southern life.

checkIn.prompt: Eli Whitney's cotton gin was supposed to make work easier. Instead, it made slavery grow. Can you think of another invention or change that ended up doing the opposite of what people expected?
checkIn.hint: Think about technology or rules that started with one goal and led somewhere very different.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from American Yawp Ch. 11 and standard 8th grade history texts.

sections.heading: The Fight for Freedom

content.simplified:
People fought against slavery from the start. The movement to end slavery was called abolitionism. The people in it were called abolitionists.

One of the most powerful voices was Frederick Douglass. He was born into slavery in Maryland. He taught himself to read in secret. In 1838, he escaped to the North. Then he did something even braver. He started telling his story to the public. He gave speeches. He wrote a famous book about his life. White people who had only heard about slavery now listened to a man who had lived it. His words changed minds.

Other abolitionists fought in different ways. William Lloyd Garrison was a white newspaper editor. He started a paper called The Liberator in 1831. He demanded that slavery end right away. Southerners hated him. One state even offered $5,000 to anyone who would arrest him.

Some abolitionists helped enslaved people escape. They built a secret network called the Underground Railroad. It was not a real railroad. It was a chain of safe houses and brave helpers. They used train words as code. A safe house was a station. A guide was a conductor. The most famous conductor was Harriet Tubman. She had escaped slavery herself. She went back south about thirteen times to lead others to freedom. She never lost a person.

Enslaved people also fought back. In 1831, an enslaved man named Nat Turner led a rebellion in Virginia. Nat Turner's Rebellion lasted two days. About sixty white people were killed. Turner was caught and hanged. After this, Southern states passed cruel new laws against enslaved people.

By the 1840s, the fight was no longer just about money. It was about whether one person could own another. Northerners and Southerners began to see each other as enemies.
content.standard:
Slavery did not go unchallenged. From the very beginning, enslaved people resisted in every way they could, and free Americans, both Black and white, fought against the system. The movement to end slavery was called abolitionism, and the people in it were called abolitionists.

One of the most powerful voices was Frederick Douglass. Douglass had been born into slavery in Maryland and taught himself to read in secret. In 1838, he escaped to the North. Then he did something that took even more courage: he started telling his story in public. He gave speeches, wrote a famous autobiography, and edited his own newspaper called The North Star. White audiences who had only heard about slavery from a distance now had to listen to a man who had lived it. His words changed minds across the country.

Other abolitionists pushed in different ways. William Lloyd Garrison was a white newspaper editor in Boston who started a paper called The Liberator in 1831. While most early abolitionists wanted to end slavery slowly, Garrison demanded it end immediately, and he refused to soften his words. Southerners hated him so much that one state offered five thousand dollars for his arrest.

Some abolitionists put their lives on the line to help enslaved people escape. They built a secret network called the Underground Railroad. It was not a real railroad. It was a chain of safe houses, hidden routes, and brave volunteers stretching from the South all the way to the free states and Canada. People used train words as code. A safe house was a station. A guide was a conductor. A person escaping was a passenger. The most famous conductor was Harriet Tubman, who had escaped slavery herself in 1849. She went back into the South about thirteen times to lead others out. She never got caught and never lost a single person.

Resistance also came from inside slavery itself. In 1831, an enslaved man named Nat Turner led the most famous slave uprising in American history. Nat Turner's Rebellion lasted only two days, but about sixty white people were killed before it was crushed. Turner was captured and hanged. Instead of making slaveholders question the system, the rebellion frightened them into making it crueler. Southern states passed harsh new laws banning enslaved people from learning to read or gathering in groups.

By the 1830s and 1840s, the gap between North and South was no longer just about money. It was about whether one human being could own another. Northerners and Southerners were starting to see each other as moral opposites. That feeling would only grow stronger as the country added new western land and had to decide, again and again, whether each new piece of it would be free or enslaved.
content.advanced:
Slavery in the United States was never uncontested. From the moment enslaved Africans arrived in colonial America, they resisted: through escape attempts, work slowdowns, sabotage, and occasional armed rebellion. Free Americans, Black and white, organized political opposition to the institution from the early national period onward. By the 1830s, this opposition had crystallized into a coherent movement called abolitionism, distinguished from earlier antislavery thought by its insistence on immediate, uncompensated emancipation rather than gradual phase out.

The most influential abolitionist voice belonged to Frederick Douglass. Born into slavery in Maryland in 1818, Douglass acquired literacy clandestinely (Southern law forbade teaching enslaved people to read), escaped to the North in 1838, and quickly became one of the most extraordinary public speakers of the century. His 1845 autobiography Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass was a literary phenomenon, so polished that some white audiences refused to believe a Black man had written it. Douglass later founded his own newspaper, The North Star, and became a transatlantic figure who spoke to packed halls in Britain and Ireland. His significance was not only rhetorical. By insisting on speaking from his own experience, he forced white Americans to grapple with slavery as something done to a person, not as an abstract economic question.

Other abolitionists pushed the movement in different directions. William Lloyd Garrison, a white newspaper editor from Massachusetts, founded The Liberator in 1831. Garrison rejected gradual emancipation and any compromise with slaveholders. His confrontational style alienated many moderates but radicalized the movement's core. Georgia offered a five thousand dollar bounty for his arrest, evidence both of his impact and of the South's view that Northern abolitionists threatened their entire social order.

The most dangerous form of abolitionist work was direct rescue. The Underground Railroad, a metaphor rather than an actual railway, was a decentralized network of safe houses, hidden routes, and volunteers (many of them free Black Americans, some of them formerly enslaved) who guided fugitives from slavery in the South to freedom in the North or Canada. The railroad's most legendary conductor, Harriet Tubman, escaped slavery in 1849 and then made approximately thirteen return trips into the South to liberate others, including members of her own family. By her own account, she never lost a person under her care, which made her, as she later put it, the most dangerous woman in America in the eyes of slaveholders.

Resistance also came from inside the system. In August 1831, an enslaved preacher named Nat Turner led approximately seventy enslaved followers in an uprising in Southampton County, Virginia. Nat Turner's Rebellion killed approximately sixty white people over two days before the militia suppressed it. Turner was captured, tried, and executed. The rebellion's significance lay less in what it accomplished, which was little, than in how slaveholders responded. Rather than reading the uprising as evidence that the slave system was unsustainable, Southern legislatures passed harsher laws: bans on teaching enslaved people to read, restrictions on Black religious gatherings, expansion of slave patrols. The rebellion, in other words, made slavery more rigidly defended, not less.

By the 1840s, the North-South divide had become a moral as well as an economic divergence. Each side increasingly described the other in language of incompatibility: the South spoke of Northern fanatics threatening Southern civilization, the North of a Slave Power conspiring against republican government. The dispute could no longer be cleanly contained by political compromise, because the participants themselves had stopped seeing it as a problem to be managed and started seeing it as a fundamental question about what kind of country the United States would be.

sections.keyIdea: Abolitionists like Frederick Douglass, William Lloyd Garrison, and Harriet Tubman pushed Americans to confront slavery as a moral issue, while events like Nat Turner's Rebellion showed that enslaved people would not stay quiet forever.

checkIn.prompt: Frederick Douglass said that telling the truth about slavery in his own voice was one of the most powerful things he could do. Why do you think a person's own story can change minds when arguments and statistics sometimes cannot?
checkIn.hint: Think about a time someone's personal story changed how you thought about something.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from American Yawp Ch. 11 and the National Park Service Underground Railroad resources.

segments.title: Road to War
segments.subtitle: How forty years of compromise finally ran out

sections.heading: Fights Over New Land

content.simplified:
For forty years, fights about slavery in Congress were really fights about land. As America grew west, each new state had to be free or slave. Both sides wanted more states on their side. More states meant more votes in the Senate.

The first big fight came in 1820. Missouri wanted to join as a slave state. That would have given slave states more votes than free states. Northerners refused. After months of arguing, Congress made a deal called the Missouri Compromise. Missouri became a slave state. Maine became a free state to balance it. A line was drawn across the rest of the Louisiana Purchase. North of the line, slavery was banned. South of the line, slavery was allowed.

The next big fight came after America won a war with Mexico in 1848. The country gained huge new lands. A Northern congressman named David Wilmot proposed a law to ban slavery in those lands. It was called the Wilmot Proviso. It passed the House but failed in the Senate. Even though it failed, it made Southern leaders angrier than ever. Some started talking about leaving the United States.

In 1850, Congress tried again. The Compromise of 1850 was a package of five laws. The North got California as a free state. The South got a tough new law called the Fugitive Slave Law. This law forced Northerners to help catch enslaved people who had escaped. People who refused could be fined or jailed.

The Fugitive Slave Law made many Northerners angry. Slavery was no longer just a Southern problem. Slave catchers came into Northern towns. Some Northerners blocked them. The country put off war for ten more years. But more and more people now thought compromise itself was the problem.
content.standard:
For the first forty years of the 1800s, almost every fight in Congress about slavery was really a fight about land. As the United States grew westward, each new state had to be either a free state or a slave state. The North and the South each wanted the count to favor them, because more states on your side meant more votes in the Senate.

The first big test came in 1820, when Missouri asked to join the Union as a slave state. That would have given slave states more votes than free states for the first time. Northern senators refused. After months of arguing, Senator Henry Clay worked out a deal called the Missouri Compromise. Missouri came in as a slave state, Maine came in as a free state to balance it, and a line was drawn at 36 degrees 30 minutes across the rest of the Louisiana Purchase. North of that line, slavery would be banned. South of it, slavery would be allowed. The deal kept the peace, but Thomas Jefferson, then in his old age, wrote that he heard it like "a fire bell in the night," warning him that the slavery question would tear the country apart someday.

The next big fight came after the United States won a war with Mexico in 1848 and took a huge piece of the West. Suddenly there was new territory the Missouri Compromise line did not cover. A Northern congressman named David Wilmot proposed a law called the Wilmot Proviso, which would have banned slavery in any land taken from Mexico. The proviso passed the House but failed in the Senate. Even though it never became law, the fight over it pushed Southern leaders to start saying out loud, for the first time, that they might leave the United States if Northerners kept attacking slavery.

Two years later, Congress tried one more time to settle things. The Compromise of 1850 was a package of five laws put together by Henry Clay. The North got California as a free state. The South got a tougher law, the Fugitive Slave Law, that required Northerners to help capture and return enslaved people who had escaped. Anyone who refused, or who helped a runaway, could be fined or jailed.

The Fugitive Slave Law backfired badly. For Northerners who had quietly looked away from slavery for years, this law put the system right in their faces. Slave catchers came into Northern cities. Free Black communities were terrified. Abolitionists organized to physically block slave catchers from taking people back south. Slavery was no longer a Southern problem. It was happening in their own towns. The country had bought itself ten more years before war, but the price was that more and more Americans now believed compromise itself was the problem.
content.advanced:
For roughly four decades, the central political question of the early American republic was not whether slavery would survive but whether it would expand. As the United States added new territory through purchase, treaty, and war, every admission of a new state forced Congress to decide whether that state would be free or slave. The stakes were structural: each new state added two senators, shifting the balance of power in the Senate and, by extension, the federal government's posture toward slavery.

The first acute crisis came in 1820. Missouri petitioned for admission as a slave state, which would have tilted the eleven-eleven Senate balance in favor of slavery for the first time. Northern senators refused. After months of acrimonious debate, Senator Henry Clay engineered the Missouri Compromise: Missouri entered as a slave state, Maine was carved out of Massachusetts and admitted as a free state to maintain parity, and a line was drawn at latitude 36 degrees 30 minutes across the remaining Louisiana Purchase territory, banning slavery north of it and permitting it south. The deal preserved the immediate balance, but Thomas Jefferson, then nearing the end of his life, recognized its deeper meaning. He wrote that the news struck him "like a fire bell in the night," a warning that the question of slavery, once made geographically explicit, could no longer be quietly contained.

The Mexican American War (1846 to 1848) reopened the wound. The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo transferred to the United States approximately half a million square miles of territory not covered by the Missouri Compromise line. Northern congressman David Wilmot proposed the Wilmot Proviso in 1846, an amendment that would have banned slavery in any territory acquired from Mexico. The proviso passed the House, where free state representation dominated, but failed in the Senate, where slave state interests held the balance. Although it never became law, the proviso fundamentally changed Southern politics. For the first time, prominent Southern leaders began to discuss secession openly as a contingency, signaling that the South was prepared to leave the Union rather than accept geographic limits on slavery's expansion.

By 1850, the question of California, which sought admission as a free state, threatened to break the country. Senator Henry Clay, by then nearing the end of his life, brokered one final compromise. The Compromise of 1850 bundled five separate measures: California's admission as a free state, the abolition of the slave trade in Washington D.C., a determination of the Texas-New Mexico border, organization of New Mexico and Utah territories under popular sovereignty, and most consequentially, a strengthened Fugitive Slave Law that compelled Northern citizens to assist in the capture and return of escaped enslaved people, with criminal penalties for refusal.

The Fugitive Slave Law was the compromise's poison pill. By legally implicating Northerners in slavery's enforcement, it transformed slavery from a distant Southern institution into a daily intrusion on Northern life. Federal commissioners conducted hearings without juries. Free Black Americans, including those who had never been enslaved, faced abduction. Abolitionists organized vigilance committees to physically prevent renditions; in cities like Boston and Syracuse, mobs rescued captured fugitives from federal custody. The Compromise of 1850 succeeded in postponing civil war by approximately a decade, but at the cost of radicalizing Northern public opinion. Each successive crisis made the next one harder to resolve, because each one had widened the audience that no longer believed compromise was possible.

sections.keyIdea: From the Missouri Compromise of 1820 through the Compromise of 1850, Congress kept patching over fights about whether new western land would be free or slave. Each compromise bought time but deepened the anger on both sides.

checkIn.prompt: Compromises are usually a good way to solve problems. Why do you think the compromises over slavery in the 1800s ended up making the country angrier instead of calmer?
checkIn.hint: Think about whether you can really compromise on a question of right and wrong.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from American Yawp Ch. 13 and standard 8th grade history texts.

sections.heading: The 1850s Explode

content.simplified:
The 1850s were the decade when everything fell apart. Each year brought a new crisis. Each crisis made the next one worse.

It started with a book. In 1852, Harriet Beecher Stowe wrote Uncle Tom's Cabin. The novel told the story of enslaved people. It sold over 300,000 copies in one year. Northerners cried over it. Southerners banned it. The book changed how many Northerners felt about slavery.

Two years later, Senator Stephen Douglas of Illinois pushed a law called the Kansas Nebraska Act. The law let the people in those territories vote on slavery themselves. Douglas called this idea popular sovereignty. He said it would solve the slavery question peacefully. It did not. The law also threw out the line drawn by the Missouri Compromise.

Both sides rushed people into Kansas to win the vote. They started fighting. By 1856, Kansas was a battlefield. About fifty people were killed. Newspapers called it Bleeding Kansas.

In 1857, the Supreme Court ruled in the Dred Scott decision. The court said Black Americans were not citizens. They had no rights. The court also said Congress could not ban slavery in the territories. To Northerners, this was a disaster. If Congress could not stop slavery in the West, what could stop it from coming to the North?

The next year, a little known lawyer named Abraham Lincoln ran against Stephen Douglas for Senate in Illinois. They held seven public debates. These are called the Lincoln Douglas Debates. Lincoln said slavery was wrong. Douglas said let the states choose. Lincoln lost the Senate race. But newspapers printed the debates. Lincoln became famous.

In 1859, an abolitionist named John Brown attacked the federal weapons storehouse at Harpers Ferry, Virginia. John Brown's Raid failed in two days. Brown was caught and hanged. But the raid scared Southerners. They began to think the whole North wanted to attack them. By the end of 1859, both sides expected war.
content.standard:
The 1850s were the decade when the country's slow burning fight over slavery finally caught fire. Each year brought a new crisis that made the next one worse.

It started with a book. In 1852, Harriet Beecher Stowe published Uncle Tom's Cabin, a novel that took readers inside the lives of enslaved people. Within a year, it had sold over 300,000 copies, an enormous number for the time. Northerners cried over the characters. Southerners called the book a lie and banned it from their states. President Lincoln supposedly later joked to Stowe that she was "the little woman who wrote the book that made this great war."

Two years later, Senator Stephen Douglas of Illinois pushed a law called the Kansas Nebraska Act. The law created two new territories and let the people living in them vote on whether to allow slavery. Douglas called this idea popular sovereignty and said it would settle the question peacefully. It did not. The law threw out the slavery ban from the Missouri Compromise that had stood for thirty four years. Both sides rushed settlers into Kansas to control the vote.

By 1856, Kansas had become a battlefield. Pro slavery Missourians crossed the border to vote illegally. Anti slavery families came in from the North. Both sides started attacking each other with guns, axes, and torches. Newspapers called it Bleeding Kansas. About fifty people died. It was a small civil war years before the big one.

Then in 1857, the Supreme Court made everything worse. In the Dred Scott decision, the court ruled that Black Americans, free or enslaved, were not citizens and had no rights under the Constitution. The court also said Congress had no power to ban slavery in the territories. To Northerners, the decision was a disaster. If Congress could not ban slavery in the West, what was to stop slavery from spreading into the free states themselves?

The next year, in Illinois, a little known lawyer named Abraham Lincoln challenged Senator Stephen Douglas in a Senate election. The two men held seven public debates, now called the Lincoln Douglas Debates. Slavery was the main topic. Douglas defended popular sovereignty. Lincoln argued that slavery was a moral wrong that had to be stopped from spreading. Lincoln lost the Senate race, but newspapers across the country printed the debates. He went from a small time politician to a national figure.

The decade ended in violence. In October 1859, a white abolitionist named John Brown led a small group of men in John Brown's Raid on the federal armory at Harpers Ferry, Virginia. He hoped to grab the weapons and hand them out to enslaved people, sparking a massive uprising. The plan failed in two days. Brown was captured and hanged. Most Northerners had not supported him. But Southern leaders saw the raid as proof that the entire North wanted to come kill them. Across the South, militias began drilling and stockpiling weapons. By the end of 1859, both sides were ready to believe the worst about each other.
content.advanced:
The 1850s were the decade in which the slow accumulation of sectional tensions exceeded the political system's capacity to manage them. Each crisis radicalized broader segments of the public. By the end of the decade, the political center had effectively collapsed.

The decade's first transformation was cultural rather than political. In 1852, Harriet Beecher Stowe published Uncle Tom's Cabin, a sentimental novel depicting the lives of enslaved people that became one of the bestselling books of the nineteenth century. By presenting enslaved people as fully realized moral and emotional beings, Stowe forced Northern readers, many of whom had treated slavery as a distant abstraction, to engage with it as a human catastrophe. Southerners denounced the book as propaganda and banned its distribution in many states, but the cultural damage to slavery's moral standing in the North was permanent. Lincoln allegedly remarked, on meeting Stowe years later, that she was "the little woman who wrote the book that made this great war."

The 1854 Kansas Nebraska Act, championed by Illinois Senator Stephen Douglas, was the political analogue. Douglas wanted to organize the Kansas and Nebraska territories to enable a transcontinental railroad through Chicago. Securing Southern support required offering something on slavery, so the act introduced popular sovereignty: territorial residents would vote on whether to permit slavery, regardless of latitude. The bill effectively repealed the Missouri Compromise's geographic ban, which had held for thirty four years. Northern outrage was immediate and structural. The Whig Party fractured beyond repair. The new Republican Party emerged, organized around opposition to slavery's expansion.

Popular sovereignty's first practical test was disastrous. Both pro and anti slavery factions rushed settlers and arms into Kansas. Missourians crossed the border to cast fraudulent ballots. Free state and slave state factions established competing territorial governments. By 1856, the territory was in open civil conflict, dubbed Bleeding Kansas by national newspapers. Approximately fifty people died in raids, retaliations, and outright battles. John Brown himself, later famous for his Harpers Ferry raid, killed five pro slavery settlers in the Pottawatomie massacre. Bleeding Kansas demonstrated, well before Fort Sumter, that the slavery question could no longer be resolved through democratic procedure.

In 1857, the Supreme Court delivered the decade's most consequential ruling. In the Dred Scott decision, Chief Justice Roger Taney held that Black Americans, whether enslaved or free, were not and could not be citizens of the United States, and that Congress lacked constitutional authority to prohibit slavery in any federal territory. The decision did more than free Dred Scott's enslavers. It seemed to nationalize slavery, raising the prospect that no free state could legally exclude slavery within its own borders. Northern Republicans responded with alarm; Southern Democrats treated the ruling as vindication. The constitutional middle ground had effectively disappeared.

The 1858 Lincoln Douglas Debates in the Illinois Senate race elevated Abraham Lincoln, then a relatively obscure Springfield lawyer, to national prominence. Across seven outdoor debates attended by enormous crowds and reprinted nationally, Lincoln pressed Douglas on the contradiction between popular sovereignty and the Dred Scott decision. Lincoln framed slavery as a moral wrong that the founders had expected to die out, an interpretation that united Northern Republicans even as Lincoln disclaimed any intention to abolish slavery where it already existed. Lincoln lost the senate race but emerged as the Republican Party's most articulate national figure.

The decade ended in armed insurrection. In October 1859, abolitionist John Brown led twenty one men in John Brown's Raid on the federal armory at Harpers Ferry, Virginia, intending to seize weapons and trigger a regional slave uprising. The plan collapsed in thirty six hours. Brown was captured by US Marines under Robert E. Lee, tried for treason, and hanged. Northern reaction was divided: most Northerners disowned Brown's methods, though some, including Henry David Thoreau, eulogized him as a martyr. Southern reaction was unified and extreme. Slaveholders interpreted the raid as confirmation that Northern abolitionists intended armed liberation, a fear that drove the South to militarize, organize state militias, and accelerate planning for secession. By the time of the 1860 election, both sides were politically and psychologically prepared for the war that followed.

sections.keyIdea: The 1850s saw one crisis after another, from Uncle Tom's Cabin to Bleeding Kansas to Dred Scott to John Brown's Raid. Each event pushed the North and South further apart and made compromise harder to imagine.

checkIn.prompt: By the end of the 1850s, both Northerners and Southerners believed the other side was the real threat. How do you think a country gets to that point, where each side stops being able to see the other as fellow citizens?
checkIn.hint: Think about how news, books, and stories shape what we believe about people we have never met.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from American Yawp Ch. 13 and the Library of Congress Civil War teaching resources.

sections.heading: The Break

content.simplified:
By 1860, the country was so divided that the presidential election had four candidates. Northerners voted for Abraham Lincoln, who said slavery should not spread. Or for Stephen Douglas, who said let each state decide. Southerners had two candidates of their own. Lincoln was not even on the ballot in most Southern states.

The Election of 1860 was won by Lincoln. He got less than forty percent of the popular vote. But he won enough free states to win the Electoral College. That was enough. He had not promised to end slavery. He only promised to stop it from spreading. But that was enough to scare the South.

Within six weeks of the election, South Carolina announced it was leaving the United States. They called it secession. By February 1861, six more states had left: Mississippi, Florida, Alabama, Georgia, Louisiana, and Texas. Together they formed a new country called the Confederate States of America. Their president was Jefferson Davis.

Lincoln did not accept that any state could leave. The Constitution did not allow it, he said. The Confederacy disagreed. They said any state could leave the Union if it wanted to.

That disagreement made war unavoidable. Talking could not solve it. Voting could not solve it. Forty years of compromise had not solved it. In April 1861, Confederate cannons fired on a federal fort in Charleston Harbor. The Civil War had begun.
content.standard:
By 1860, the country was so divided that the presidential election split into four candidates. Voters in the North chose between Abraham Lincoln, the Republican who said slavery should not spread, and Stephen Douglas, the Democrat who said let each state decide. Voters in the South had two candidates of their own, both committed to defending slavery. Lincoln did not even appear on the ballot in most Southern states.

The Election of 1860 was won by Lincoln. He took less than forty percent of the national popular vote, but he won enough free states to take the Electoral College, and that was all that mattered. He had not promised to end slavery. He had promised only to keep it from spreading. But that was enough to terrify the South.

Within six weeks of the election, before Lincoln was even sworn in, South Carolina announced it was leaving the United States. They called it secession, the formal withdrawal of a state from the country. By February 1861, six more states had followed: Mississippi, Florida, Alabama, Georgia, Louisiana, and Texas. Together, they wrote a new constitution and formed the Confederate States of America, with Jefferson Davis as their president.

Lincoln, now president, refused to accept that any state had the right to leave. The Constitution, he said, did not allow it. The federal government could not just disappear because some states did not like an election result. The Confederacy disagreed. To them, the Union was a partnership of states, and any state could walk away from a partnership it had freely joined.

That disagreement, more than any single law or compromise or speech, is what made war unavoidable. Talking could not settle it. Voting could not settle it. Compromise had been tried for forty years and had only made things worse. In April 1861, Confederate forces fired on a federal fort in Charleston Harbor, and the Civil War began. But the question of why it happened goes back much further than that single shot. It goes back to a country that tried for decades to be half free and half slave, and finally found out that it could not.
content.advanced:
The 1860 presidential election was effectively four separate contests. The Democratic Party, broken on the question of slavery's protection in the territories, fielded two candidates: Stephen Douglas as the candidate of Northern Democrats committed to popular sovereignty, and John C. Breckinridge as the candidate of Southern Democrats demanding federal protection of slavery in all territories. The Constitutional Union Party, drawing border state moderates, nominated John Bell on a platform of preserving the Union and dodging the slavery question. The Republican Party nominated Abraham Lincoln on a platform opposing slavery's expansion while explicitly disclaiming any intent to interfere with slavery in states where it already existed. Lincoln did not appear on the ballot in ten of the eleven states that would later secede.

The Election of 1860 was won by Lincoln with less than forty percent of the popular vote, distributed entirely across the North and the West. He carried the Electoral College decisively, in part because the South's split between Breckinridge and Bell had divided the opposition. Lincoln's victory represented a structural reality the South had long feared: the Northern population had grown sufficient to elect a president without any Southern support, and the federal government could now be controlled by an explicitly antislavery coalition.

Southern leaders interpreted the result as a categorical threat. Lincoln had not promised abolition, but he had said clearly that slavery was a moral wrong and had voiced confidence that, contained from expansion, it would die out over time. To the slaveholding South, that constituted a long term sentence of economic and social annihilation. South Carolina announced its secession on December 20, 1860, just six weeks after the election. Mississippi, Florida, Alabama, Georgia, Louisiana, and Texas followed by February 1861, before Lincoln was even inaugurated. The seven states convened in Montgomery, Alabama, to draft a constitution and elect Jefferson Davis as president of the Confederate States of America. After Fort Sumter, four more states (Virginia, North Carolina, Tennessee, Arkansas) would secede, bringing the total to eleven.

Lincoln's response, articulated most fully in his First Inaugural Address in March 1861, rejected secession's legal foundation. The Constitution, he argued, was not a contract among independent states but a perpetual union of one people. No state had the right to leave unilaterally. The federal government could no more disappear at the option of dissatisfied minorities than any state government could disappear at the option of its discontented citizens. Lincoln pledged to maintain federal authority where it remained, including the few federal installations still in seceded states.

The Confederacy's counterposition was equally clear. The Union was a compact among sovereign states. States had freely entered the compact and could freely depart from it. The Confederate constitution explicitly protected slavery and asserted state sovereignty in language modeled, somewhat ironically, on the original American framing.

The disagreement over secession's legality, more than any single dispute over slavery's expansion, was what made war structurally inevitable. The two positions could not be reconciled by debate, vote, or compromise, because they were claims about what kind of entity the United States fundamentally was. Either it was a permanent nation that could not be dissolved by its constituent states, or it was a contractual association from which states could withdraw. The question could not be answered by argument. It would have to be answered by force.

That answer began on April 12, 1861, when Confederate batteries opened fire on Fort Sumter. But the deeper origin of the war predated Fort Sumter by decades. The country had attempted, since its founding, to be half free and half slave. The compromises that maintained that arrangement had gradually exhausted themselves, replaced by a politics in which both sides increasingly believed compromise itself was complicity. The war did not begin in 1861 so much as become unavoidable in the years before it.

sections.keyIdea: Lincoln's election in 1860, even though he was not an abolitionist, convinced Southern states that the federal government had turned against them. Eleven states seceded and formed the Confederate States of America. Lincoln refused to accept secession, and the disagreement led directly to civil war.

checkIn.prompt: The Southern states said they had a right to leave the Union the way they had once chosen to join it. Lincoln said no state had that right, because the country was permanent, not just a club. Whose argument do you find more convincing, and why?
checkIn.hint: Think about what it means for a country to be permanent versus what it means for it to be a free agreement among states.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from American Yawp Ch. 14 and Lincoln's First Inaugural Address.

segments.title: The War Itself
segments.subtitle: How four years of fighting transformed a country

sections.heading: The War Begins

content.simplified:
On April 12, 1861, Confederate cannons fired on Fort Sumter. The fort was a federal base in Charleston Harbor. A small Union force was inside. After 34 hours of bombing, the Union surrendered. No soldier was killed in the fighting. But the Civil War had begun.

Both sides thought the war would be short. Both sides were wrong. Most people thought one big battle would end it. Instead, the war lasted four years. It killed more Americans than any war before or since.

The two presidents were very different. Abraham Lincoln was the new Republican president. He had grown up poor and taught himself law. He had won the 1860 election without a single Southern electoral vote. He had little military experience. Jefferson Davis was the new Confederate president. He had been a US senator and Secretary of War. He had military experience but little patience for politics.

The North had big advantages. It had about 22 million people. The South had only about 9 million people, including 3.5 million enslaved people. The North had most of the factories, railroads, and ships. But the South had better generals at the start. Robert E. Lee was the most famous. Lincoln offered him command of the Union army. Lee said no. He could not fight against his home state, Virginia.

The Union's plan was called the Anaconda Plan. It was named after the snake that crushes its prey. The plan was to blockade Southern ports, take control of the Mississippi River, and slowly squeeze the South into surrender. People made fun of the plan at first. It sounded too slow. But in the end, it was almost exactly what won the war.
content.standard:
On April 12, 1861, Confederate cannons in Charleston Harbor opened fire on Fort Sumter, a small federal fort held by a tiny Union garrison. After 34 hours of bombardment, the Union surrendered. No soldier was killed in combat. But the Civil War had begun, and within weeks the country was at war with itself.

Both sides expected a short war. Both sides were wrong. Most Americans on both sides believed one big battle would settle everything. Instead, the war would last four years and kill more Americans than any war before or since.

The two presidents could not have been more different in personality. Abraham Lincoln, the new Republican president, had grown up poor in Kentucky and Indiana, taught himself law, and won the 1860 election without a single Southern electoral vote. He was a careful thinker and a great speaker, but he had little military experience. Jefferson Davis, the new Confederate president, had served as a US senator and Secretary of War. He had military experience but little patience for politics, and he often clashed with Confederate state governors who refused to share resources.

The two sides had very different advantages. The North had about 22 million people, while the South had only about 9 million, including 3.5 million enslaved people. The North also had most of the country's factories, railroads, and ships. The South had less of all of that, but it had something the North did not: most of the best generals in the prewar US Army had been Southerners. The most famous of them, Robert E. Lee, was offered command of the Union army by Lincoln himself. Lee turned it down and went south to defend Virginia.

The Union's overall strategy was called the Anaconda Plan, named after the snake that crushes its prey. It called for blockading Southern ports to cut off trade, capturing the Mississippi River to split the Confederacy in two, and slowly squeezing the South into surrender. The plan was mocked at first because it sounded too slow. In the end, it was almost exactly what won the war.
content.advanced:
On April 12, 1861, Confederate batteries under General P.G.T. Beauregard opened fire on Fort Sumter, a federal installation occupying an island in Charleston Harbor that had been resupplied but not reinforced after South Carolina's secession. The garrison, commanded by Major Robert Anderson, surrendered after thirty four hours of bombardment. No combatant was killed during the engagement itself; the only fatality was a Union soldier killed in an accidental explosion during the surrender salute. But the bombardment crystallized what had been ambiguous for months. The federal government and the Confederacy were now at war.

The war that followed defied the expectations of nearly everyone who began it. Both sides anticipated a brief conflict. Northern volunteers signed three month enlistments. Southern leaders predicted European recognition within a year. Instead, the war lasted four years and produced approximately 750,000 military deaths, more than every other American war combined until that point. It became, in scale and in violence, the largest conflict in the Western world between the Napoleonic Wars and the First World War.

The two presidents brought sharply contrasting strengths and limitations. Abraham Lincoln, born in 1809 in a Kentucky log cabin and largely self educated, had served briefly in Congress and built a successful Springfield law practice before winning the presidency. He had no significant military experience. He compensated through extraordinary political skill, an unusually capacious moral imagination, and an ability to manage difficult subordinates that few of his generals shared. Jefferson Davis, by contrast, was a West Point graduate, a Mexican War veteran, a former United States senator, and a former Secretary of War under President Pierce. He understood military matters as well as Lincoln did not. But Davis was rigid in temperament, slow to delegate, and chronically at odds with Confederate governors who interpreted state sovereignty to mean their states' resources should not be commandeered for the broader war effort.

The strategic balance heavily favored the Union. The North had a population of approximately 22 million; the Confederacy had about 9 million, of whom 3.5 million were enslaved. The North contained roughly 90 percent of the nation's manufacturing capacity, two thirds of its railroad mileage, and the great majority of its banking, shipping, and merchant infrastructure. The South possessed the offsetting advantages common to defenders fighting on familiar ground: shorter supply lines, knowledge of terrain, and the strategic luxury of needing only to survive rather than to conquer. It also retained a disproportionate share of the prewar US Army's most accomplished officers, including Robert E. Lee, whom Lincoln offered command of the Union forces in Virginia. Lee declined; despite personal opposition to slavery and to secession, he could not, by his own account, raise his sword against his native Virginia. He resigned his federal commission and accepted a Confederate command, a decision that would prolong the war by years.

Union strategy emerged in the form of the Anaconda Plan, proposed by General in Chief Winfield Scott in 1861 and named for the constricting snake. The plan had three components: a naval blockade of Southern ports to choke off cotton exports and military imports, control of the Mississippi River to split the Confederacy and isolate its western states, and gradual military pressure that would force a negotiated surrender without invading the Southern heartland. The plan was widely mocked in the Northern press for being too patient, particularly as early Union land campaigns produced humiliating defeats at Bull Run and elsewhere. In the end, however, the war was won almost exactly along the lines Scott had drawn. The blockade strangled the Southern economy. Vicksburg gave the Union the Mississippi. Sherman's later campaigns supplied the constricting pressure. The Anaconda Plan was the strategic skeleton of Union victory, even as it was being publicly derided as inadequate.

sections.keyIdea: The Civil War began with the Confederate attack on Fort Sumter in April 1861. Both sides expected a short war. The Union had more people, factories, and railroads, while the Confederacy had more skilled generals at the start, including Robert E. Lee.

checkIn.prompt: Robert E. Lee was offered command of the Union army by Lincoln but went with the Confederacy because he could not raise his sword against Virginia. What does that choice tell you about how Americans of his time thought about loyalty to a state versus loyalty to the country?
checkIn.hint: Think about whether "American" meant the same thing to people in 1861 as it does today.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from American Yawp Ch. 14 and the National Park Service Civil War resources.

sections.heading: Turning Points

content.simplified:
The first year of the war went badly for the Union. Confederate generals like Robert E. Lee and Stonewall Jackson outsmarted Union commanders again and again. New weapons made the fighting brutal. The Minie ball was a new type of bullet that could hit a target from 500 yards away. Old style charges across open ground became deadly. Nurses like Clara Barton worked on battlefields under fire to help the wounded.

The first turning point came in September 1862. Lee invaded the North. He hoped a big victory would get Britain and France to help the South. Union forces stopped him at the Battle of Antietam in Maryland. Over 22,000 men were killed or wounded in twelve hours. It was the bloodiest single day in American history. Lee retreated.

Five days later, Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation. It said that on January 1, 1863, all enslaved people in Confederate states would be free. The proclamation could not actually free anyone behind Confederate lines. But it changed the war. Now any Union advance was also a freedom march. The proclamation also let Black men join the Union Army. They formed the USCT, the United States Colored Troops. Nearly 180,000 Black men served.

The second turning point came in July 1863. Two big things happened at once. In Pennsylvania, Lee invaded the North a second time. The Battle of Gettysburg lasted three days. Over 50,000 men were killed or wounded. On the last day, Lee ordered 12,000 men to charge across open ground. They were cut to pieces. Lee retreated. The Confederacy never invaded the North again.

Four months later, Lincoln gave the Gettysburg Address at the cemetery for the Union dead. It was only 272 words long. But it changed how Americans thought about the war.

The very next day, July 4, 1863, the Confederate stronghold at Vicksburg, Mississippi, surrendered. The Siege of Vicksburg had lasted 47 days. Union General Ulysses S. Grant led it. The Union now controlled the entire Mississippi River. The Confederacy was split in two.

That same month, the 54th Massachusetts, one of the first Black regiments, attacked Fort Wagner in South Carolina. About half its soldiers were killed or wounded. The attack failed. But it proved Black men would fight for their freedom.

In 1864, Lincoln put Grant in command of all Union armies. Grant brought General William T. Sherman to lead the campaign in the South. Sherman captured Atlanta. This helped Lincoln win re-election against the Copperheads, Northern Democrats who wanted peace right away. Then Sherman launched his March to the Sea. He used a strategy called total war, which attacks not just soldiers but also the resources armies need. His army destroyed about $100 million in property across Georgia. The South was running out of time.
content.standard:
The first year and a half of the war went badly for the Union. Confederate generals like Robert E. Lee and Stonewall Jackson outmaneuvered Union commanders again and again. New weapons made the fighting brutal. The Minie ball, a new type of bullet that expanded when fired, could hit a target accurately from 500 yards away. Old fashioned charges across open ground became suicidal. Volunteer nurses like Clara Barton worked on battlefields under fire to save the wounded. Even so, casualties piled up faster than anyone had imagined.

The first turning point came in September 1862. Lee invaded the North, hoping a big victory on Northern soil would convince Britain and France to support the South. Union forces blocked him at the Battle of Antietam in Maryland. The fighting on September 17, 1862 killed or wounded over 22,000 men in twelve hours, the bloodiest single day in American history. The battle ended in a draw, but Lee retreated. Lincoln seized the moment.

Five days after Antietam, Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation. It declared that on January 1, 1863, all enslaved people in Confederate states would be free. The proclamation could not actually free anyone behind Confederate lines, but it changed the war forever. Now any Union advance was also a freedom march. And it kept Britain and France from supporting the South, since neither wanted to be seen defending slavery. The proclamation also opened the door for Black men to enlist in the Union Army. Soon they would form what was called the USCT, the United States Colored Troops. Nearly 180,000 Black men served before the war was over.

The second turning point came in July 1863, in two places at once. In Pennsylvania, Lee's second invasion of the North met disaster at the Battle of Gettysburg. Three days of fighting left over 50,000 casualties, the most of any battle ever fought on American soil. On the third day, Lee ordered 12,000 men to charge across open ground at the Union center. They were cut to pieces. Lee retreated, and the Confederacy never invaded the North again. Four months later, Lincoln went to Pennsylvania to dedicate the cemetery for the Union dead and gave the Gettysburg Address, a 272 word speech that redefined the war as a fight for human equality.

The very next day, July 4, 1863, the Confederate stronghold at Vicksburg, Mississippi, surrendered after a 47 day Siege of Vicksburg by Union General Ulysses S. Grant. The Union now controlled the entire Mississippi River. The Confederacy was split in two. Combined with Gettysburg, July 1863 was the moment the Confederacy lost its hope of winning the war.

That same month, the 54th Massachusetts, one of the first official Black regiments, led an assault on Fort Wagner in South Carolina. About half its soldiers were killed or wounded. The attack failed, but the regiment's bravery while the country watched made it impossible to deny that Black men would fight for their own freedom.

By 1864, Lincoln had finally found his fighter. He put Grant in command of all Union armies. Grant brought General William T. Sherman to lead the campaign in the South. Sherman captured Atlanta in September 1864, helping Lincoln win re-election against the Copperheads, Northern Democrats who wanted an immediate peace. Then Sherman launched his famous March to the Sea, pioneering the strategy of total war, which attacks not just enemy soldiers but the resources and infrastructure that armies need. His army destroyed an estimated $100 million in property across Georgia. The South was running out of soldiers, food, and time.
content.advanced:
The early war went disastrously for the Union. Confederate commanders, particularly Lee and Stonewall Jackson, repeatedly outmaneuvered larger Union armies through superior tactical coordination and a willingness to take operational risks. The technological transformation of warfare added a layer of horror that neither side had anticipated. The Minie ball, a conical soft lead bullet that expanded upon firing to grip the rifling of a musket barrel, gave infantry roughly five times the effective range of older smoothbore weapons. Frontal assaults across open terrain, the standard tactical doctrine inherited from Napoleonic warfare, became catastrophic. Volunteer nurses including Clara Barton (later founder of the American Red Cross) worked on battlefields under fire to evacuate the wounded. Casualties exceeded the imagination of every prewar planner.

The first strategic turning point came in September 1862. Lee invaded Maryland, calculating that a Confederate victory on Northern soil might secure European recognition and break Northern political will. Union forces under General George McClellan, having captured Lee's battle plans almost by accident, blocked the invasion at the Battle of Antietam. The fighting of September 17, 1862, produced approximately 22,000 casualties in twelve hours, more than the combined American casualties of the Revolutionary War, the War of 1812, and the Mexican American War. The battle was tactically a draw, but Lee retreated to Virginia, allowing Lincoln to claim a victory sufficient for his next political move.

Five days after Antietam, Lincoln issued the preliminary Emancipation Proclamation, with the final version effective January 1, 1863. The proclamation declared all enslaved people in territory still in rebellion against the United States to be "thenceforward, and forever free." Its immediate practical effect was limited; the federal government could not enforce emancipation in territory it did not yet control. But its strategic effect was decisive. The proclamation transformed every advance of the Union army into an act of liberation, foreclosed any possibility of British or French recognition (since neither European power could be seen defending slavery), and authorized Black enlistment in the Union army. The USCT, the United States Colored Troops, would eventually field nearly 180,000 Black soldiers, approximately ten percent of total Union forces. Many had been enslaved themselves.

The decisive twin turning points came in July 1863. In Pennsylvania, Lee's second invasion of the North culminated in the three day Battle of Gettysburg, the largest battle ever fought on American soil. Over 50,000 men were killed, wounded, or captured. On the third day, Lee ordered approximately 12,000 men under General George Pickett to assault the Union center across nearly a mile of open ground; fewer than half returned. Lee, watching the survivors stagger back, reportedly told them, "It is all my fault." The Confederate army retreated to Virginia. It never invaded the North again. Four months later, Lincoln traveled to Pennsylvania to dedicate the cemetery for the Union dead and delivered the Gettysburg Address, a 272 word speech that, in approximately two minutes, redefined the war as a struggle to vindicate the Declaration of Independence's claim that all men are created equal, and as a test of whether democratic government could endure.

The day after Gettysburg's third day (July 4, 1863), the Confederate stronghold at Vicksburg, Mississippi, surrendered to Ulysses S. Grant after a 47 day Siege of Vicksburg. With Vicksburg's fall, the Union controlled the entire Mississippi River. The Confederacy was split in two; Texas, Arkansas, and most of Louisiana were severed from the Confederate heartland east of the river. Together, Gettysburg and Vicksburg constituted the strategic inflection point of the war. After July 1863, Confederate victory required not just military defense but a political collapse in the North that became increasingly improbable.

Also that month, the 54th Massachusetts, one of the first official Black regiments, assaulted Fort Wagner in Charleston Harbor. The attack failed; approximately half the regiment's soldiers were killed, wounded, or captured. But the 54th's documented heroism, in particular the death of Colonel Robert Gould Shaw beside his men, accelerated public acceptance of Black military service and recruitment for the USCT.

By 1864, Lincoln had restructured Union command. He elevated Grant to general in chief of all Union armies, finally finding a commander whose strategic temperament matched the war's demands. Grant entrusted operations in the western theater to William T. Sherman. Sherman captured Atlanta in September 1864, an achievement of enormous political consequence: it secured Lincoln's reelection against the Democratic challenger George McClellan, whose Copperhead faction had advocated immediate negotiated peace, possibly with concessions on slavery. Sherman then launched his March to the Sea, pioneering what became known as total war, which directs military force not only at enemy soldiers but at the agricultural, industrial, and transportation infrastructure that supports them. His army destroyed an estimated 100 million dollars in property across Georgia, ripping up railroads, burning supplies, and demonstrating that the Confederate government could not protect its own population. By early 1865, the Confederacy was running out of soldiers, food, money, and political legitimacy. The end was now a matter of when, not whether.

sections.keyIdea: Antietam, Gettysburg, and Vicksburg were turning points that broke the Confederacy's chances of winning. The Emancipation Proclamation transformed the war into a fight to end slavery and opened the door for nearly 180,000 Black soldiers to serve. By 1864, Grant and Sherman were closing in on the South.

checkIn.prompt: Sherman believed war should be ugly enough that people would never want to fight one again. Total war attacks not just soldiers but the resources and infrastructure that armies need. Do you think that strategy was justified to end the war faster, or did it cross a line? Why?
checkIn.hint: Think about who is harmed in a total war strategy and what the alternatives might have been.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from American Yawp Ch. 14, the National Park Service Civil War resources, and the Civil War Battle Simulation primary source archive.

sections.heading: The End, and What Came After

content.simplified:
By the spring of 1865, Lee's army was starving and trapped. Grant had chased him through Virginia all winter. On April 9, 1865, Lee met Grant at a farmhouse at Appomattox Court House to surrender. Lee said, "There is nothing left for me to do but to go and see General Grant."

Grant could have humiliated Lee. He did not. He gave generous terms. Confederate soldiers could go home with their horses. Officers could keep their swords. Grant even shared his food with the hungry Confederate troops. When Union soldiers started celebrating with cannons, Grant told them to stop. "The war is over," he said. "The rebels are our countrymen again."

Five days later, on April 14, 1865, John Wilkes Booth shot Lincoln at Ford's Theatre. Lincoln died the next morning. The country was in shock. Vice President Andrew Johnson became president. He had little sympathy for Black rights. Lincoln's plans for a kind peace were now in different hands.

In December 1865, the states approved the 13th Amendment to the Constitution. It ended slavery in the entire United States. The Emancipation Proclamation had been a wartime order. It only freed people in Confederate states. The 13th Amendment was permanent law. About four million people who had been enslaved became free, everywhere in the country.

The years that followed were called Reconstruction. Reconstruction lasted from 1865 to 1877. Its goal was to rebuild the South and to make formerly enslaved people full citizens. There were real wins. The 14th and 15th Amendments gave Black Americans citizenship and voting rights. Black congressmen were elected from Southern states for the first time. Schools were built for Black children.

But Reconstruction also faced violent attacks from white Southerners. By 1877, federal protection for Black Southerners was taken away. The wins of Reconstruction were rolled back over the next decades.

The Civil War answered some big questions. It answered whether a state could leave the Union. It could not. It answered whether slavery would last. It would not. But the question of what freedom would really mean for four million people was left for later. In some ways, the country is still answering it today.
content.standard:
By the spring of 1865, Lee's army was starving and trapped. Grant had pursued him relentlessly through Virginia all winter. On April 9, 1865, Lee met Grant in the parlor of a small farmhouse at Appomattox Court House to surrender. Lee said, "There is nothing left for me to do but to go and see General Grant, and I would rather die a thousand deaths."

Grant could have humiliated Lee. He did not. He offered generous terms. Confederate soldiers could go home with their horses. Officers could keep their swords. Grant even shared his rations with the hungry Confederate troops. When Union soldiers started celebrating with cannon fire, Grant ordered them to stop. "The war is over," he said. "The rebels are our countrymen again." Grant's generosity at Appomattox set the tone for what came next, or at least it was supposed to.

Five days after the surrender, on April 14, 1865, John Wilkes Booth shot Lincoln during a play at Ford's Theatre in Washington. Lincoln died the next morning. The country, which had just begun to relax after four years of war, was thrown back into shock. Lincoln's death changed everything about what came next. Vice President Andrew Johnson took over. He had grown up poor in Tennessee and disliked the Southern aristocracy, but he had little sympathy for Black rights. The plans Lincoln had been making for a generous peace, with strong protections for formerly enslaved people, were now in the hands of someone far less committed to those protections.

In December 1865, eight months after Lincoln's death, the country took the most important step of all: the states ratified the 13th Amendment to the Constitution, abolishing slavery throughout the entire United States. The Emancipation Proclamation had been a wartime order that only freed enslaved people in Confederate states. The 13th Amendment was permanent law. About four million people who had been enslaved became legally free, everywhere in the country, for good. It was the most fundamental change in American law since the Constitution itself.

The years that followed were called Reconstruction. Reconstruction lasted from 1865 to 1877. Its goal was to rebuild the South after the war and to make formerly enslaved people full citizens of the United States. There were real successes: the 14th and 15th Amendments granted Black Americans citizenship and voting rights. Black congressmen were elected from Southern states for the first time. Schools were built for Black children. But Reconstruction also faced violent resistance from white Southerners who refused to accept the new order. By 1877, federal protection for Black Southerners was withdrawn, and the gains of Reconstruction were rolled back over the decades that followed.

The Civil War answered some questions and left others unanswered. It answered, finally, whether one part of the United States could leave when it lost an election. It could not. It answered whether slavery would survive in America. It would not. But the deeper question of what freedom would mean for four million formerly enslaved people, and what the country owed them as fellow citizens, was a question the war did not finish answering. That question is in some ways still being answered today.
content.advanced:
By the spring of 1865, the Confederate position had become militarily untenable. Lee's Army of Northern Virginia, starving and reduced to perhaps 28,000 effectives, attempted to break west and link up with remaining Confederate forces in North Carolina. Grant pursued relentlessly through the spring of 1865, with Union cavalry under Philip Sheridan racing ahead to cut off Lee's escape routes. By April 9, Lee was surrounded near a small Virginia town called Appomattox Court House. Recognizing the inevitability of surrender, Lee reportedly said: "There is nothing left for me to do but to go and see General Grant, and I would rather die a thousand deaths."

Grant's surrender terms set a deliberate tone. Confederate soldiers were paroled and allowed to return home, retaining their horses (essential for spring planting) and personal sidearms. Officers kept their swords as a gesture of respect. Grant ordered his army's quartermasters to share Union rations with the hungry Confederate troops, who had not eaten reliably in weeks. When Union artillery batteries began firing salutes to celebrate the surrender, Grant ordered them to stop, telling his staff: "The war is over. The rebels are our countrymen again." The choices made at Appomattox, deliberately framed to facilitate national reconciliation, would shape the next half decade of American politics, though not always as Grant intended.

Five days after the surrender, on April 14, 1865, John Wilkes Booth, a Confederate sympathizer and well known stage actor, shot President Lincoln during a performance of "Our American Cousin" at Ford's Theatre in Washington. Lincoln died the following morning. The country, having barely begun to absorb the war's end, was plunged into renewed shock. The political consequences were immediate and far reaching. Vice President Andrew Johnson, a Tennessee Democrat who had remained loyal to the Union but who held conventionally white supremacist views, became president. Lincoln had been laying plans for a relatively lenient Reconstruction designed to restore Southern political institutions while protecting freedpeople through federal supervision. Johnson would prove far less committed to those protections, and his clashes with the Republican Congress would shape the entire Reconstruction era.

The most consequential legislative achievement of the war's aftermath came in December 1865. The states ratified the 13th Amendment to the Constitution, which abolished slavery and involuntary servitude throughout the entire United States, with one narrow exception for criminal punishment. The Emancipation Proclamation had been a wartime executive order limited to states in active rebellion; the 13th Amendment was permanent constitutional law. Approximately four million formerly enslaved people gained legal freedom, including those in border states (Maryland, Kentucky, Missouri, Delaware) where slavery had remained legal throughout the war. The amendment represented the most fundamental alteration to the American constitutional order since the Constitution's original ratification.

The years between 1865 and 1877 are collectively called Reconstruction, an attempt to rebuild the South politically and economically while integrating freedpeople as full citizens. The constitutional framework expanded significantly: the 14th Amendment (1868) established birthright citizenship and equal protection under the law, and the 15th Amendment (1870) prohibited racial discrimination in voting. Federal occupation of the South enabled a remarkable, if brief, period of biracial democracy. Black Americans were elected to Congress and to state legislatures, including, for the first time, from former Confederate states. Public schools were established for Black children where none had existed before. The Freedmen's Bureau provided food, education, and legal advocacy.

The achievements of Reconstruction, however, were undone by sustained violent resistance. White paramilitary organizations including the original Ku Klux Klan terrorized Black voters and politicians. Northern political will to enforce Reconstruction eroded as the country grappled with economic depression and the political costs of indefinite military occupation. The disputed presidential election of 1876 produced a compromise (the Compromise of 1877) that effectively withdrew federal troops from the South in exchange for Republican Rutherford B. Hayes assuming the presidency. With federal protection withdrawn, Southern state governments systematically reversed Reconstruction's gains, establishing Jim Crow segregation, disenfranchisement, and a sharecropping system that approximated slavery's economic relations without its formal legal structure. The protections of the 14th and 15th Amendments would not be meaningfully enforced until the civil rights movement nearly a century later.

The Civil War answered, definitively, two questions that had hung over the early republic. It answered, by the force of arms, that no state could unilaterally leave the Union; the country was permanent, not contractual. It answered, by constitutional amendment, that slavery was incompatible with American law and would no longer be tolerated within American borders. But the war could not, and did not, answer the deeper questions it raised. What would freedom actually mean, in practice, for four million formerly enslaved people? What did the nation owe them as citizens? What did equal protection actually require, and how would it be enforced? Those questions were left to Reconstruction, then to Jim Crow, then to the civil rights movement, and in significant ways are still being answered. The war ended in 1865, but the country it left behind has never quite finished settling what the war was actually about.

sections.keyIdea: Lee surrendered to Grant at Appomattox in April 1865, ending the war. Five days later, Lincoln was assassinated. The 13th Amendment ended slavery permanently across the country. Reconstruction tried to rebuild the South and integrate formerly enslaved people as citizens, with both real successes and major failures.

checkIn.prompt: The Civil War answered some big questions, like whether states could leave the Union and whether slavery would survive. But it left other questions unanswered, like what freedom would mean for four million formerly enslaved people. Why do you think wars sometimes settle questions and sometimes only open new ones?
checkIn.hint: Think about the difference between making something illegal and changing how people actually treat each other.

sections.sourceNote: Adapted from American Yawp Ch. 14, the National Park Service Civil War resources, and the Civil War Battle Simulation.
